questions.question: What is the primary role of the **CLR (CoreCLR)** when you run a .NET application?
options: It is the managed virtual machine that loads assemblies, JIT-compiles IL to native code, runs the garbage collector, and enforces type safety
It is the command-line tool that restores NuGet packages and invokes MSBuild to produce assemblies
It is the set of compilers (Roslyn) that translate C# source into IL at build time
It is the base class library of types such as `System.Text.Json`, `HttpClient`, and `Span<T>`
questions.explanation: The CLR is the managed runtime: it loads assemblies, JIT-compiles IL, runs the GC, and enforces type safety. The dotnet CLI and Roslyn are SDK/build-time tools, and the listed types belong to the BCL, which is a separate layer that ships with the runtime.

questions.question: Why can the **same published (non-AOT) .NET assembly** run on both x64 and Arm64 machines?
options: The assembly contains CPU-agnostic IL, and RyuJIT compiles that IL to the target machine's native instructions at run time
The assembly contains both x64 and Arm64 native code, and the loader picks the matching one
The CLR emulates an x64 CPU on Arm64 hardware so the same native code always runs
Roslyn detects the host architecture during build and emits architecture-neutral native code
questions.explanation: A managed assembly ships IL plus metadata, not native code; RyuJIT specializes that IL for the actual CPU at run time. There is no per-architecture native code in the artifact and no CPU emulation. Native AOT, by contrast, is per-RID native and not portable.

questions.question: Under the .NET release cadence, which statement about support tiers is correct?
options: Even-numbered releases are LTS with 3 years of support; odd-numbered releases are STS with 18 months
Even-numbered releases are STS with 18 months; odd-numbered releases are LTS with 3 years
Every release is supported for 3 years regardless of whether its number is even or odd
LTS and STS releases both ship every November but LTS gets 18 months and STS gets 3 years
questions.explanation: Even-numbered .NET releases are LTS (3 years of support); odd-numbered releases are STS (18 months). The other options invert the tiers or the support windows.

questions.question: A build server can run a published .NET app, but `dotnet build` fails with a "no SDK" error. What is the most likely cause?
options: The machine has only the .NET runtime installed; building requires the SDK, which adds the compilers, MSBuild, and the CLI
The machine has the SDK but is missing the runtime, which is needed to compile IL
The `runtimeconfig.json` has roll-forward disabled, which blocks the build step
The project targets `netstandard2.0`, which cannot be built without an STS runtime
questions.explanation: The runtime (CoreCLR + BCL) only runs apps; building needs the SDK, which is a superset adding Roslyn, MSBuild, and the dotnet CLI. The other options misattribute the failure to runtime config or TFM choice.

questions.question: Since .NET 6, the minimal hosting model lets `Program.cs` use top-level statements, and the separate `Startup.cs` with `ConfigureServices`/`Configure` is no longer used by the default template.
questions.explanation: The minimal hosting model (since .NET 6) collapses service registration and pipeline configuration into a single top-level `Program.cs`; `Startup.cs` and an explicit `Main` are gone from the template (though you can still hand-roll a Startup-like split).

questions.question: How does **tiered compilation** give both fast startup and high steady-state throughput?
options: Methods are first compiled quickly at Tier 0 with minimal optimization; hot methods are later recompiled in the background at Tier 1 with full optimizations
All methods are compiled at full optimization on first call, then cold ones are recompiled at a cheaper tier to save memory
Tier 0 runs the IL through an interpreter while Tier 1 JITs everything ahead of the first call
Tier 0 compiles on the build machine and Tier 1 compiles on the target machine at run time
questions.explanation: Tier 0 is a fast quick-JIT for snappy startup; hot methods get recompiled at Tier 1 with full optimizations in the background. The other options reverse the tiers or invent an interpreter / build-time split that does not exist here.

questions.code:
for (int i = 0; i < 1_000_000_000; i++)
    Work(data[i % data.Length]);
questions.question: A method enters this billion-iteration loop at Tier 0 and never returns until the loop finishes. Which runtime feature ensures the loop still gets fully optimized, and how?
options: On-Stack Replacement (OSR) swaps in the Tier 1 optimized version while the method is still executing on the stack
Dynamic PGO promotes the method to Tier 1 only after it returns, so the loop runs optimized on its next call
ReadyToRun pre-JITs the loop at publish time so it always starts at Tier 1
The garbage collector pauses the loop and triggers a recompile during the next GC
questions.explanation: Without OSR, a method that enters a long loop at Tier 0 never returns to be promoted and runs un-optimized for the whole loop. OSR patches in the Tier 1 version mid-stack. PGO/R2R/GC do not address an in-progress long-running method.

questions.question: What does **dynamic PGO** do, and what is its default status in modern .NET?
options: It instruments Tier 0 code to record real execution data (hot types, taken branches) that Tier 1 uses to devirtualize and inline; it is on by default since .NET 8
It profiles the app during the build and bakes the profile into the assembly; it must be opted into per project
It collects production telemetry and uploads it to optimize future SDK releases; it is off by default for privacy
It precompiles all reflection-heavy code paths to native at publish; it is on by default only for Native AOT
questions.explanation: Dynamic PGO instruments Tier 0 to capture run-time behavior (which types hit a virtual call, which branches are taken) so Tier 1 can devirtualize, inline, and reorder; it is on by default since .NET 8. The other options describe build-time or telemetry mechanisms that are not dynamic PGO.

questions.question: You must ship a CLI tool to customer machines that may not have .NET installed, and cold-start latency dominates the experience. Which publish mode best fits?
options: Native AOT â€” a self-contained native executable, no runtime needed, fastest startup and smallest footprint
Framework-dependent â€” smallest artifact, relying on a shared runtime the customer already has
Plain JIT with tiered compilation left on so dynamic PGO can optimize the hot path
ReadyToRun, framework-dependent â€” pre-JIT at publish while keeping startup dependent on the host runtime
questions.explanation: Native AOT produces a self-contained native binary with no runtime dependency and the fastest cold start and smallest footprint, which matches a CLI tool where startup dominates and .NET may be absent. Framework-dependent and R2R-framework-dependent still need a runtime on the host; plain JIT pays a cold-JIT startup cost.

questions.question: What does the `*.runtimeconfig.json` file shown here control for a published app?
options: Which framework version the host rolls forward to, plus runtime knobs like GC mode and tiered compilation
The list of every assembly the app loads and the probing paths used to find them
The NuGet package versions restored into the build across the whole repository
The set of source files and `#if` symbols used when Roslyn compiles the project
questions.explanation: `runtimeconfig.json` tells the host which framework version to roll forward to and carries runtime knobs (GC mode, tiering, thread-pool settings). The assembly list and probing paths live in `deps.json`; package versions belong in CPM; source/compile settings are MSBuild concerns.

questions.question: Because trimming (`PublishTrimmed=true`) removes unused IL using static analysis, code that relies on reflection over types the analyzer cannot see can fail at run time, and Native AOT implies trimming.
questions.explanation: Trimming is static-analysis-based, so reflection the analyzer can't trace may have its targets removed and break at run time unless annotated or marked trim-safe. Native AOT requires trimming, which is why heavy reflection is restricted under AOT.

questions.question: Which statements about **framework-dependent (FDD)** vs **self-contained (SCD)** deployment are correct? Select all that apply.
options: FDD produces the smallest artifact because it ships only your code and relies on a shared runtime on the host
SCD bundles the runtime for a specific RID, so it runs on a machine with no .NET installed
SCD requires specifying a RID (e.g. `-r linux-x64`), whereas FDD does not
FDD bundles the runtime so the host never needs .NET installed
SCD always produces a smaller artifact than FDD because trimming is mandatory
questions.explanation: FDD is smallest and depends on a shared runtime; SCD bundles the runtime per-RID (so it needs `-r`) and runs where .NET is absent. Option 4 describes FDD as bundling the runtime (that is SCD), and option 5 is wrong because SCD is the largest model and trimming is optional, not mandatory.

questions.question: In the minimal hosting model, what is the correct order of the standard startup flow for a web app?
options: `WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args)` â†’ register services on `builder.Services` â†’ `builder.Build()` â†’ configure the pipeline â†’ `app.Run()`
`builder.Build()` â†’ `WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args)` â†’ register services â†’ configure the pipeline â†’ `app.Run()`
Register services on `builder.Services` â†’ `WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args)` â†’ configure the pipeline â†’ `builder.Build()` â†’ `app.Run()`
`WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args)` â†’ `builder.Build()` â†’ register services on `app.Services` â†’ configure the pipeline â†’ `app.Run()`
questions.explanation: The flow is always: create the builder, register services on `builder.Services`, call `builder.Build()`, configure the request pipeline, then `app.Run()`. Services must be registered before `Build()`, so the options that build first or register after building are wrong.

questions.question: When **Central Package Management (CPM)** is enabled for a repository, where do package versions live and how do project files reference packages?
options: Versions live in a single `Directory.Packages.props` (with `ManagePackageVersionsCentrally=true`); project `PackageReference` entries omit the version
Versions live in each project's `.csproj`; `Directory.Packages.props` only lists which packages are allowed
Versions live in `runtimeconfig.json`; project files reference packages by RID instead of version
Versions live in a `packages.config` per project; `Directory.Build.props` overrides them globally
questions.explanation: CPM centralizes all versions in `Directory.Packages.props` (via `ManagePackageVersionsCentrally=true`) using `PackageVersion` entries, so per-project `PackageReference` entries are versionless. The other options misplace versions in runtimeconfig, csproj, or the legacy packages.config.

questions.question: Compared with the default JIT deployment, which characteristics specifically apply to a **Native AOT** build? Select all that apply.
options: There is no JIT and no IL at run time; the whole app is compiled to native at publish
Reflection and dynamic code are restricted, and trimming is required
It cannot benefit from run-time dynamic PGO specializing code for the live workload
It produces the smallest binary while still requiring the shared runtime to be installed on the host
It keeps full reflection-emit capability so any reflection-heavy library works unchanged
questions.explanation: Native AOT compiles entirely at publish (no JIT/IL at run time), restricts reflection/dynamic code, requires trimming, and has no run-time PGO. Option 4 is wrong because AOT is self-contained and needs no runtime on the host; option 5 is wrong because reflection-emit is exactly what AOT gives up.

questions.question: As of mid-2026, a long-lived production service targets `net9.0`. What is the correct senior recommendation and the reason behind it?
options: Move to `net10.0`, because .NET 9 (STS) ends support 2026-11-10 while .NET 10 is the current LTS supported to 2028
Stay on `net9.0`, because STS releases receive the same 3-year support window as LTS once GA
Move to `net11.0` immediately, because it is the current LTS with the longest support window
Move to `netstandard2.0`, because it never goes out of support and runs everywhere
questions.explanation: .NET 9 is STS and ends support 2026-11-10, so it is about to fall out of support; .NET 10 is the current LTS (supported to 2028-11-14), the right target for a long-lived service. .NET 11 is STS (odd), and `netstandard2.0` is a library portability contract, not a runtime to host an app on.

questions.question: A senior framing says "C# is compiled twice." Which description of those two compilations is accurate?
options: Ahead of time by Roslyn (C# â†’ IL) on the build machine, then just in time by RyuJIT (IL â†’ native) on the running machine
Once by RyuJIT into IL on the build machine, then once by Roslyn into native code at run time
Twice by Roslyn â€” first to IL, then to native â€” both during the build, with no run-time compilation
Once by the SDK into a ReadyToRun image and once by the CLR into IL when the process starts
questions.explanation: Roslyn compiles C# to IL ahead of time on the build machine; RyuJIT compiles that IL to native just in time on the running machine. The second compile is what lets one IL artifact run optimally on many CPUs. The other options swap the tools' roles or eliminate the run-time JIT step.
